---
title: "quiz6"
title-block-style: none
format: live-html
engine: knitr
sidebar:
  style: docked
resources:
  - soccer_spi.csv
  - training_scores.csv
  - music_tracks.csv
  - game_publishers.csv
---

{{< include /_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}
{{< include /_extensions/r-wasm/live/_gradethis.qmd >}}

<script>
document.addEventListener("DOMContentLoaded", () => {
  const params = new URLSearchParams(window.location.search);
  const showQ = params.get("q");

  // Show only the requested section
  if (showQ) {
    document.querySelectorAll("section[id]").forEach(section => {
      if (section.id !== showQ) section.style.display = "none";
    });
  }

  // Hide page title
  document.querySelectorAll(".quarto-title-block")
    .forEach(el => el.style.display = "none");

  // Hide ALL section titles (## ...)
  document.querySelectorAll("section[id] > h2")
    .forEach(h => h.style.display = "none");
});
</script>


## Q1 — One-sample z-test on SPI (σ known, manual) {#q1a}
Using `soccer_spi.csv`, test whether the average SPI equals 70.

- $H_0: \mu = 70$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 70$

Assume the population standard deviation is known: $\sigma = 12$.  
Use a **two-sided z-test**.

**Compute the z statistic.**
```{webr}
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

mu_0 <- ___
sigma <- ___
n <- ___
xbar <- ___

z <- ___
z
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

mu_0 <- 70
sigma <- 12
n <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x)

z <- (xbar - mu_0) / (sigma / sqrt(n))
z
# -0.8637
```

## Q1 — One-sample z-test on SPI (σ known, manual) {#q1b}
Using `soccer_spi.csv`, test whether the average SPI equals 72.

- $H_0: \mu = 72$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 72$

Assume the population standard deviation is known: $\sigma = 12$.  
Use a **two-sided z-test**.

**Compute the z statistic.**
```{webr}
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

mu_0 <- ___
sigma <- ___
n <- ___
xbar <- ___

z <- ___
z
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

mu_0 <- 72
sigma <- 12
n <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x)

z <- (xbar - mu_0) / (sigma / sqrt(n))
z
# -2.3544
```

## Q2 — One-sample z-test on SPI (σ known, using BSDA) {#q2a}
Using `soccer_spi.csv`, test whether the average SPI equals 70.

- $H_0: \mu = 70$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 70$

Assume the population standard deviation is known: $\sigma = 12$.  
Use `z.test()` with a two-sided alternative.

**Compute the two-sided p-value.**
```{webr}
library(BSDA)
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

z.test(x,
       mu = ___,
       sigma.x = ___,
       alternative = "two.sided"
)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
library(BSDA)
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

z.test(x,
  mu = 70, 
  sigma.x = 12, 
  alternative = "two.sided"
)
# 0.3878
```
## Q2 — One-sample z-test on SPI (σ known, using BSDA) {#q2b}
Using `soccer_spi.csv`, test whether the average SPI equals 72.

- $H_0: \mu = 72$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 72$

Assume the population standard deviation is known: $\sigma = 12$.  
Use `z.test()` with a two-sided alternative.

**Compute the two-sided p-value.**
```{webr}
library(BSDA)
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

z.test(x,
       mu = ___,
       sigma.x = ___,
       alternative = "two.sided"
)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
library(BSDA)
df <- read.csv("soccer_spi.csv")
x <- df$spi

z.test(x,
  mu = 72, 
  sigma.x = 12, 
  alternative = "two.sided"
)
# 0.0186
```

## Q3 — One-sample t-test on SPI (σ unknown, manual; n = 20) {#q3a}
Using `training_scores.csv`, test whether the average training performance score equals 75.

- $H_0: \mu = 75$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 75$

The population standard deviation is unknown. Use the sample standard deviation and perform a **two-sided one-sample t-test**.

**Compute the t statistic.**
```{webr}
df <- read.csv("training_scores.csv")
x <- df$Performance_Score

mu_0 <- ___
n <- ___
xbar <- ___
s <- ___

t_stat <- ___
t_stat
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
df <- read.csv("training_scores.csv")
x <- df$Performance_Score

mu_0 <- 75
n <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x)
s <- sd(x)

t_stat <- (xbar - mu_0) / (s / sqrt(n))
t_stat
# 0.4070
```

## Q3 — One-sample t-test on SPI (σ unknown, manual; n = 20) {#q3b}
Using `training_scores.csv`, test whether the average training performance score equals 72.

- $H_0: \mu = 72$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 72$

The population standard deviation is unknown. Use the sample standard deviation and perform a **two-sided one-sample t-test**.

**Compute the t statistic.**
```{webr}
df <- read.csv("training_scores.csv")
x <- df$Performance_Score

mu_0 <- ___
n <- ___
xbar <- ___
s <- ___

t_stat <- ___
t_stat
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
df <- read.csv("training_scores.csv")
x <- df$Performance_Score

mu_0 <- 72
n <- length(x)
xbar <- mean(x)
s <- sd(x)

t_stat <- (xbar - mu_0) / (s / sqrt(n))
t_stat
# 1.9312
```

## Q4 — One-sample t-test on Track.Score (σ unknown, built-in) {#q4a}
Using `music_tracks.csv`, test whether the average track score equals 45.

- $H_0: \mu = 45$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 45$

Assume **σ is unknown**. Use `t.test()` with a two-sided alternative.

**Compute the two-sided p-value.**
```{webr}
df <- read.csv("music_tracks.csv")
x <- df$Track.Score

t.test(x,
       mu = ___,
       alternative = ___
)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
df <- read.csv("music_tracks.csv")
x <- df$Track.Score

t.test(x,
       mu = 45, 
       alternative = "two.sided"
)
# 0.1359
```
## Q4 — One-sample t-test on Track.Score (σ unknown, built-in) {#q4b}
Using `music_tracks.csv`, test whether the average track score equals 47.

- $H_0: \mu = 47$  
- $H_1: \mu \ne 47$

Assume **σ is unknown**. Use `t.test()` with a two-sided alternative.

**Compute the two-sided p-value.**
```{webr}
df <- read.csv("music_tracks.csv")
x <- df$Track.Score

t.test(x,
       mu = ___,
       alternative = ___
)
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
df <- read.csv("music_tracks.csv")
x <- df$Track.Score

t.test(x,
       mu = 47, 
       alternative = "two.sided"
)
# 0.1532
```
## Q5 — Proportion test: chi-square to z (prop.test) {#q5a}
Using `game_publishers.csv`, test whether the proportion of games published by Nintendo equals 0.023.

- $H_0: p = 0.023$  
- $H_1: p \ne 0.023$

Use a two-sided `prop.test()`.

`prop.test()` reports a chi-square statistic with 1 degree of freedom.  
For a 1-df test, $\chi^2 = z^2$.

**Compute $z = \sqrt{\chi^2}$.**
```{webr}
vg <- read.csv("game_publishers.csv")

x <- sum(vg$Publisher == "Nintendo")
n <- nrow(vg)

prop.test(x = ___,
          n = ___,
          p = ___,
          alternative = ___,
          correct = FALSE
)

z = sqrt(___)
z
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
vg <- read.csv("game_publishers.csv")

x <- sum(vg$Publisher == "Nintendo")
n <- nrow(vg)

prop.test(x = x,
          n = n,
          p = 0.023,
          alternative = "two.sided",
          correct = FALSE
)

z = sqrt(7.9901)
z
# 2.8267
```

## Q5 — Proportion test: chi-square to z (prop.test) {#q5b}
Using `game_publishers.csv`, test whether the proportion of games published by Nintendo equals 0.061.

- $H_0: p = 0.061$  
- $H_1: p \ne 0.061$

Use a two-sided `prop.test()`.

`prop.test()` reports a chi-square statistic with 1 degree of freedom.  
For a 1-df test, $\chi^2 = z^2$.

**Compute $z = \sqrt{\chi^2}$.**
```{webr}
vg <- read.csv("game_publishers.csv")

x <- sum(vg$Publisher == "Nintendo")
n <- nrow(vg)

prop.test(x = ___,
          n = ___,
          p = ___,
          alternative = ___,
          correct = FALSE
)

z = sqrt(___)
z
```

```{webr}
#| echo: false
#| output: false
vg <- read.csv("game_publishers.csv")

x <- sum(vg$Publisher == "Nintendo")
n <- nrow(vg)

prop.test(x = x,
          n = n,
          p = 0.061,
          alternative = "two.sided",
          correct = FALSE
)

z = sqrt(1.7547)
z
# 1.3247
```
